title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered to teach digital skills to the elderly. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Bridging the Gap: Digital Literacy Volunteers

texts.A:
### Sam, The Tablet Workshop
'I thought I’d be teaching complex apps, but we started with how to turn the device on,' Sam says. [51]The hardest part was the repetitive nature of the instructions, which required a level of patience I didn't know I had.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my own tech-related stress decreased, as I learned to explain things simply.[/48] [53]I learned that technology is a vital tool for combating social isolation among the elderly.[/53] [54]The highlight for me was a 90-year-old student who finally made a video call to her grandson in Australia.[/54] [50]I realized that my background in customer service was perfect for handling the students' initial frustrations.[/50] [47]Initially, the slow pace was a shock to my system, but I adapted.[/47] It was a humbling experience.
texts.B:
### Elena, The Coding for Seniors Class
Elena taught basic HTML to a group of retirees. 'It was like learning a new language together,' she explains. [47]I hadn't expected the physical strain of bending over multiple desks for hours to affect my back so much.[/47] [49]The experience taught me that curiosity has no age limit and that learning is a lifelong necessity.[/49] [56]If I hadn't seen a request for help on my university’s community board, I never would have volunteered.[/56] [48]I noticed that my own coding skills sharpened as I had to deconstruct complex concepts into simple steps.[/48] [50]I’ve found that the organizational skills I used in my job as a librarian were essential for managing the class materials.[/50] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep friendships that would form between the students and me.
texts.C:
### Marcus, The Smartphone Clinic
Marcus ran drop-in sessions at a local library. 'It was chaos at first,' he notes. [51]We struggled with the wide variety of devices, which made it hard to give a single set of instructions.[/51] [54]I was amazed by the resilience of the students, who were often terrified of 'breaking' the internet.[/54] [48]I noticed that my own patience levels reached an all-time high, which was a great personal development I hadn't planned for.[/48] [53]I realized that digital literacy is a fundamental right in the modern world, not a luxury.[/53] [55]It took a while to adjust to the noise and constant interruptions of a busy library environment.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried that nobody would show up, but the sessions were always packed.
texts.D:
### Sophie, The Cybersecurity Seminar
Sophie gave talks on how to avoid online scams. 'The stakes were high,' she says. [47]I hadn't expected the emotional weight of hearing stories from people who had lost their savings to affect me so deeply.[/47] [56]I wouldn't have known about the need for this if my own grandfather hadn't been a victim of a scam.[/56] [53]The experience taught me that knowledge is the best defense against digital predatory behavior.[/53] [48]I noticed that my public speaking confidence increased significantly through the weekly seminars.[/48] [55]I found the initial task of simplifying complex security protocols incredibly frustrating and time-consuming.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that the students would find the topic too scary, but they were empowered by the information.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or emotional impact of the volunteering environment?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) mentions the physical strain on her back, and Sophie (D) mentions the emotional weight of hearing scam stories.

questions.question: notes an unexpected personal or skill-related improvement that resulted from teaching?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) saw his tech stress decrease, Elena (B) sharpened her coding, and Sophie (D) increased her speaking confidence.

questions.question: expresses a change in their outlook regarding the nature of learning or societal rights?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) learned that curiosity has no age limit, and Marcus (C) saw digital literacy as a fundamental right.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) used his customer service skills, and Elena (B) used her librarian's organizational skills.

questions.question: describes a specific technical or logistical difficulty during the sessions?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions the repetitive nature of instructions, and Marcus (C) the variety of devices.

questions.question: admits to having had prior concerns or having an unexpected emotional connection?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) formed deep friendships, and Marcus (C) worried nobody would show up.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about technology or knowledge?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) saw tech as a tool against isolation, and Sophie (D) saw knowledge as the best defense.

questions.question: highlights the value of a specific moment of success or the character of the students?
questions.explanation: Sam (A) mentions the student making a video call, and Marcus (C) the resilience of his students.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the environment or task?
questions.explanation: Marcus (C) adjusted to the library noise, and Sophie (D) found simplifying protocols time-consuming.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external trigger or personal connection that led them to volunteer?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) saw a university board request, and Sophie (D) was motivated by her grandfather's experience.
